item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I quite liked this. However, I have to ask, how much research did you do for this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the kind of stuff that's right up my alley - genuinely mysterious, not overblown, just plausible enough, nags at the back of your mind. Can't get enough of these articles, when they're done well like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Definitely like this one. This is a very good 'unsolved mystery' SCP - it provides enough information to intrigue, without throwing in a bunch of stuff to merely confuse. It's too clear that there's a deliberate purpose, but there's just not enough to to hone in on what that purpose is, while having enough to speculate on. The use of a legitimately mysterious undersea noise to build off of it was also fantastic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would imagine it's "beyond [the southern hemisphere]"- as in, cetaceans in the northern hemisphere may also be affected.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
though cetacean populations in the southern hemisphere and beyond may become distressed.

What do you mean by "and beyond"?

item.title: Regarding the recent page revision
item.content:encoded: Just an FYI, I went ahead and changed the audio player from Flash to HTML5, as Flash is rapidly losing support and already can't be viewed by many people, including Firefox and Mobile users. No content changes were made, and the article is otherwise unchanged.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this. It epitomizes the overall nature of the Foundation.

It demonstrates quite succinctly the aspect of "We have so many dangerous things to worry about, so even though this thing is strange and clearly anomalous and the explanation of what exactly it is could be earth-shattering, we don't have the time or the resources to worry about it or try to explain it because it isn't dangerous."

The note at the end really makes it. It has great personality, you can feel the quiet desperation and frustration.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this thing because I want to know what the hell it is. It seems to be linked to an item on mars, but no real link can be found. Its a mystery, and a low priority one too. and I feel that you play on that very well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is fresh as hell. +1. The world's a crazy-ass place and not everything has to make sense.

Also it gives me hella vibes of His Master's Voice by Stanislaw Lem and that was cool too

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Come to earth! We have crab cakes!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this, for the reasons mentioned above, and also because it's one of my favorite types of SCPs: One that offers no closure, no explanation — just a few dangling clues that come tantalizingly short of anything conclusive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As I was reading I kept waiting for the big plot twist, but the lack of a real resolution was more unsettling than any revelation.

+1

(Also the fact that the audio recording vaguely seemed like humming gave me the heebie-jeebies)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for the kind words, and for helping me with the draft!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Loved this in the chat and I love it here. What turns this from an article I would downvote to an article I would upvote is the note from the Director - this SCP is pretty boring and low-priority, but it's self aware about it and uses that fact to its advantage.

You've also of subverted the trope of "secret note to the O5s explaining boring SCP is actually super dangerous!!!" by making the note solidify the object's lack of importance. The note has such personality and it conveys a story really well - you can feel the head researcher's frustration with his higher-ups and his helplessness in solving the object's mysterious qualities.

As it is, this SCP is a really solid example of worldbuilding in the Foundationverse. Upvoted :)

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
I wrote this 'cause I'm waiting to be employed.

Thanks to Decibelle, Joreth, Dog Teeth, Updog, Tuomey, Failure, AdrienLane, and phyre for the feedback! I just really want to make sure, you know?

Antenna image is from here, Mars image, with some edits, is from here. Audio file is from here. All media files are in the public domain.

Please ask me how much research I did for this.
